data.title: Lightwave Logic's Ultra-Fast Modulators Deserve A Spot On Your Watchlist
data.description:
Lightwave Logic, Inc. (NASDAQ:LWLG) is a tech firm that develops electro-optic (EO) polymers to enhance the performance of high-speed data transmission in artificial intelligence (AI). However, EO also helps speed up quantum computing and even next-generation communications. LWLG's EO polymers basically enable ultra-fast, low-power modulation that can be integrated into existing semiconductor processes. The demand for optical components is projected to be at $100 billion by 2030, and the company expects to enter this market with its modulators integrated into communication chips and photonic systems. For this integration, LWLG is working in collaboration with Polariton Technologies and Advanced Micro Foundry.

Moonshot On EO Polymer

Lightwave Logic is a technology company that develops next-generation electro-optic (EO) polymers. As previously noted, the idea is that EO polymers can potentially improve the data transmission speeds by also optimizing power consumption. Having said that, LWLG was founded back in 1991 and is currently headquartered in Englewood, Colorado. And since then, they still remain pre-product-revenues, which also shows how challenging it has been to actually deliver a working, viable product. However, if LWLG's EO concept succeeds, it would pave the way for enhanced AI, quantum computing, data, and space communications.

Having said that, it's undeniable that the demand for optical components, like LWLG's EO polymers, is projected to grow rapidly to about $100 billion by 2030. This represents a massive market opportunity, especially relative to LWLG's tiny market cap of just $105.0 million. The way these materials work is by being used in modulators that convert electrical signals into optical signals. This matters because EO polymer technology enables upgrading data speed and bandwidth in smaller devices.

Therefore, with LWLG's it's theoretically possible to fit even faster data speed transmission into smaller components. And by extension, these EO polymers can be integrated into chips with existing semiconductor manufacturing processes like CMOS, InP, and GaAs. In fact, LWLG already holds over 70 patents supporting its EO polymers platform. And indeed, I believe there's an immense amount of previous R&D effort that supports their current objective with EO polymers, unlike other proof-of-concept ideas by competitors.

Beyond chips, LWLG also has solutions for quantum network applications. In this area, LWLG is working with SilOriX and the Karlsruhe Institute of Technology to develop a low-power modulator designed for latency-sensitive applications. In other words, this type of modulator would be ideal for quantum networks and supercomputers with low-voltage and low-temperature requirements.

Moreover, LWLG's roadmap includes a first stage focused on the development of materials and technology. As of 2024, they've announced that this initial stage was finalized with their new generation of the EO polymers platform. Looking forward, their second phase will be completed by 2026. This second stage will focus more on actually fostering a potential customer base for its product integration in silicon chips. For this, they'll use existing production methods already formalized in stage 1.

After stage 2, the company should be ready to shift from an R&D firm to a commercial one by launching a viable product with its EO polymers. So, unfortunately, it does mean that LWLG still seems years away from generating any product revenues. Still, I like that LWLG manufactures its EO modulators using its proprietary platform. For this, they do resort to partners like Advanced Micro Foundry and Polariton Technologies AG, which are in charge of integrating these modulators into communication chips. And ultimately, those chips are part of optical transceivers or photonic integrated circuits [PICs] that are widely used in servers, routers, AI clusters, and cloud infrastructure.

But if LWLG's technology succeeds, its EO modulators could enhance the performance of those PIC chips by making them faster with reduced power consumption. Note that LWLG's second stage also aims to serve clients that run massive AI data centers, which is a rapidly growing market as well. And as you might imagine, EO polymers and modulators could be critical for additional (even if only marginal) gains for ultra-fast data transmission.

Still, remember that LWLG's investment thesis ultimately lies in its third stage, which is scheduled for 2026. At that point, management believes they'll be ready for mass production, as well as diversifying into adjacent markets and applications that can also benefit from its EO technology. But just to give you an idea, AI data centers can be optimized with the EO polymers.

For example, NVIDIA's (NVDA) AI Factory, which has tenfold more interconnections than traditional data centers, naturally has an extremely high power consumption. In this sense, LWLG's technology would be a potential solution for this kind of data center because it reduces voltage, increases the bandwidth, and lowers the energy per bit transferred. And when you consider the massive scale of computation that AI centers require, it's quickly evident that optimizing at the per-bit level can lead to massive improvements at scale.

In fact, LWLG targets the 800 Gbps, 1.6 Tbps, and 3.2 Tbps data communication segments as their largest revenue opportunity. This would fill the gap above 200 Gbps, where other technologies can become problematic. In other words, LWLG's technology could become useful for all kinds of higher data transmission speeds in data centers that go beyond NVDA's AI. It could be an ideal fit for Meta (META), cloud infrastructure like AWS (AMZN), Google Cloud (GOOG), and high-performance telecom networks in general.

From Lab to Market: Stage 2 Commercialization

Per LWLG's recent SEC filing, they've made progress toward their Stage 2 (2025-2026) goals. This means LWLG is already pivoting toward product integration and commercialization. They're collaboratively developing bespoke modulators for target market uses like a 1.6 Tbps transceiver for AI servers with Polariton Technologies. After all, this seems to be an immediate need in this sector due to the demand for high-speed data links in AI clusters. Besides, for this AI cluster market, LWLG is designing polymer slot modulators on the silicon platform of its partner, Advanced Micro Foundry. These modulators are applicable to 800 Gbps and 1.6 Tbps transceivers.

It's also worth noting that any initial small-batch production is probably going to be in LWLG's Colorado facility. I imagine that if their technology is a success, and they receive larger orders, they're probably going to rely a bit more on their foundry partners (like Advanced Micro Foundry), which are outside the US. This is why their recent March 28 announcement was promising. It seems their Process Design Kit [PDK] now also enables integration of the EO polymers into silicon photonic integrated circuits (Si-PICs) using a Back-End-of-Line (BEOL) process compatible with standard semiconductor fabrication.

In other words, their PDK is portable, which means that US semiconductor fabs should be able to quickly integrate it and scale it up as well if needed. So, LWLG seems perfectly compatible with the US's strategic interest of onshoring chip production through the Chips Act incentives, and could even act as an added secular tailwind in the bullish scenario. After all, US-based companies could quickly receive more efficient modulators. And the fact that LWLG has already been implemented with two semiconductor foundries is very promising, as it could accelerate its eventual commercialization efforts.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, LWLG is a microcap of just $105.0 million. Their latest 10-K shows their balance sheet holds $27.7 million in cash, essentially no financial debt. In total, their recent book value stood at $33.4 million, indicating a somewhat reasonable P/B of 3.1. For comparison, their peers' P/B is 3.1 as well, so it appears fairly valued by this metric. And as previously mentioned, they have no product revenues, only negligible licensing and royalty revenues of $81.9 thousand in 2024. They also recorded approximately $13.8 thousand in revenues from device processing work for a customer.

But overall, LWLG is still far away from generating meaningful revenues. Also, I estimate their 2024 cash burn was $17.9 million. Note that I got this figure by adding their cash flows from operations and CAPEX. And this suggests a runway of roughly 1.6 years at the current burn rate, which is another concern since LWLG's investment thesis is definitely long-term.

I imagine LWLG is actively looking for larger deals to incorporate their EO polymers, which align with their recent strategic partnerships. However, the reality is that so far, there are no confirmed commercial customers yet. Plus, as you might expect, this is a highly competitive field, with several other better-capitalized players that are exploring other potential solutions. For example, competitors may develop materials that scale up better or are more reliable than polymer modulators. And until we see LWLG hit industrial production, its underlying tech still remains highly speculative.

Beyond these considerations, I do believe LWLG is technically promising. And they've made some tangible progress that adds some credence to their lofty goals. But ultimately, we have to wait and see how they progress with their stage 2 roadmap. Also, they recently changed their CEO after many years of high promises and lackluster stock performance. This could be a good sign, as the new CEO could bring a renewed focus on actually shipping a successful EO polymer product. In the end, I believe this technology could be highly disruptive and revolutionary, but for now, it remains a moonshot. So, make sure you size your position accordingly.

Conclusion: Neutral On This Long-Term Moonshot

Overall, I believe LWLG is indeed promising from a purely technical perspective. EO polymers could unlock significant optimizations that, at scale, generate substantial efficiencies across AI, data centers, cloud computing, and even quantum computing. Unfortunately, LWLG is still a very speculative bet with a long road ahead until it even reaches its initial commercialization efforts. And when you factor in their relatively short cash runway, I think the prudent rating is a "Hold" for now. However, I certainly believe LWLG is worth adding to your watchlist because of its interesting technology. But until we see something more concrete, I remain neutral on LWLG as a potential investment.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Invesco Developing Markets Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. As the result of a reorganization on May 24, 2019, the returns of the fund for periods on or prior to May 24, 2019 reflect performance of the Oppenheimer predecessor fund. Share class returns will differ from the predecessor fund due to a change in expenses and sales charges. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore, performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • The first quarter was volatile, with mixed results across equity markets. China, the largest emerging market economy, posted a strong positive performance despite the overhang of US tariffs. Indian equities disappointed again with a second consecutive quarterly decline. Emerging Eastern Europe and Latin America had strong positive performance for the quarter, despite apparent uncertainty about the Trump administration’s effect on the Ukraine conflict and tariffs on Mexico. Though we anticipate continued market volatility amid uncertain US policy, we believe emerging market equities remain attractive to investors because they are mispriced, with valuation discounts hovering at 40% and 30% relative to US and global equities, respectively.
  • Emerging market equity performance depends primarily on two factors, China and the US dollar, both of which have been considerable headwinds for a long time. In our view, this may be an inflection point that could usher in another structural bull market in emerging market equities as these longtime headwinds may be turning into tailwinds that could underpin potentially significant gains. We are increasingly bullish on a cyclical recovery in China. Meanwhile, "US exceptionalism" has come under pressure, with the dollar under considerable strain.

As of 03/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

We use a bottom-up, benchmark agnostic approach. Sector and country exposures are a by-product of stock selection. Sector and industry weights reflect areas where historically we tend to find extraordinary companies, identify dynamic changes and see real value creation. Those areas include technology, consumer, health care, financials and resources/industrials. As of quarter end, relative to the MSCI EM Index, the fund was overweight in health care, consumer discretionary, industrials and real estate, while underweight in financials, information technology, materials, utilities, consumer staples, energy and communication services.

During the quarter, we initiated positions Latin American commerce and financial technology company MercadoLibre (MELI), Indian bank ICICI (IBN) and Chinese biotech WuXi AppTec (OTCPK:WUXIF). We sold Pernod Ricard (OTCPK:PDRDF), Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone, Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF), Global Unichip and BIM Birlesik (OTCPK:BMBRF).

We added to the following existing positions:

Alibaba (BABA) is a Chinese technology and e-commerce company and one of the world's largest retailers.

PDD (PDD) is a Chinese e-commerce company that pioneered the successful group purchase model. Meituan is the world's largest on-demand food delivery service provider and China's largest e- commerce platform for in-store dining services.

AIA is a Hong Kong-based multinational insurance and finance corporation. It is the largest publicly listed life insurance group in the Asia-Pacific region.

Daiichi Sankyo (OTCPK:DSKYF) is a global pharmaceutical company and Japan’s second-largest pharmaceutical firm, with established research and development locations in the US and Europe as well as key emerging markets.

We trimmed the following positions:

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is one of the world's leading semiconductor foundries and the key enabler of the new computing revolution.

Fomento Economico Mexicano (FEMSA) operates Latin America’s leading convenience store chain and beverage company.

Kotak Mahindra Bank is among India’s largest private banks with operations in banking, asset management, life insurance and capital markets.

Tencent is a Chinese internet and technology company with a robust suite of digital offerings and is a long-term fund holding.

Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) is a South Korean company that produces semiconductors, along with consumer electronic products, industrial equipment and internet access network systems.

Performance highlights

In our view, the real reason to invest in developing market equities is the opportunity to capture unique companies with durable long-term growth and sustainable advantages. Our investment approach typically avoid companies with governance conflicts, unsustainable advantages, mean reversion, product cycle/gadgets and capital intensive/cyclical industries.

From a sector perspective, stock selection in financials added the most to relative return. An underweight in IT and stock selection in consumer staples also added to relative results. The largest detractor from relative return was stock selection in consumer discretionary, followed by stock selection in real estate and industrials.

Geographically, an underweight in Taiwan was the largest contributor to relative return. An overweight in Mexico and lack of exposure to Thailand also contributed to relative return. The largest detractor from relative return was an underweight and stock selection in China. Stock selection and an underweight in South Africa and stock selection in the Philippines also detracted from relative return.

Contributors to absolute performance

Tencent, a long-term fund holding, is a Chinese internet and technology company that has developed a robust suite of digital offerings for its user base. Tencent provides communication and social tools, PC and mobile games, and a plethora of digital content, including news and literature.

Kotak Mahindra Bank is among India’s largest private banks with operations in banking, asset management, life insurance and capital markets. India’s financial sector has several structural trends that have underpinned its strong growth and returns on investment.

H World Group is a pioneer in China’s hotel industry, primarily using a franchise model. It has solidified and maintained its leadership position through investments in infrastructure technology, which have supported operational efficiency and boosted brand loyalty.

Detractors from absolute return

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is one of the world's leading semiconductor foundries and the key enabler of the new computing revolution, with multiple architectures, chip platforms and design teams competing to push computing and artificial intelligence innovation.

Swiggy operates a food and grocery delivery business in India. Alongside its competitor Zomato (not a fund holding), Swiggy is one half of a duopoly in India’s grocery and food delivery markets.

Oberoi Realty is a real estate development company operating in Mumbai and focused on premium developments. The company’s focus is residential projects, but it also has a diversified portfolio of projects that target the upper end of the market segment.

Invesco Developing Markets Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Best Stock With A Catalyst Competition: The Winners
data.description:
In March, Seeking Alpha invited analysts to participate in a Best Stock With A Catalyst competition. We received 44 submissions. Here's a look at all of the winners as determined by the judging editors:

1) Early Retiree – United Internet: Multiple Revaluation Catalysts Ahead - This is an under the radar stock that trades at a significant discount to its sum of the parts with catalysts for earnings growth and multiple expansion, an asymmetric risk/reward profile and a clear mispricing.

2) Max Helfert – TransMedics: The Financial Tipping Point That Will Drive Mass Adoption - The shift to a full-service logistics platform has created a high-growth, high-barrier business model, resulting in strong revenue growth and profitability while it is revolutionizing organ transplantation, improving outcomes while reducing long-term healthcare costs.

3) IB's Thoughts – Abeona Therapeutics: A Buy With Major PDUFA Catalyst On April 29 That Should Send Shares Higher - The analyst said its strong cash position and promising pipeline present a high-reward investment, and that if Pz-cel wins approval, shares would trade significantly higher - which it did, and the stock jumped significantly from the date of publication to the high on May 1 (before the recent pullback).

We selected the winners based on how compelling the thesis is (50%) and independent insights (50%).

First place receives an award of US $1,500, second place US $1,250, and third place US $1,000.

Thank you to everyone who participated - we certainly enjoyed reading your submissions. For those who didn't place, don't worry, we’ll post another competition in the near future. Stay tuned for more details.

data.title: Western Asset Total Return Unconstrained Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Economic uncertainties triggered a flight to quality during the first quarter of 2025, driving U.S. Treasury yields lower and their prices higher. The economy continued to expand, while inflation remained higher than the Federal Reserve’s (“Fed”) 2% target. Meanwhile, President Trump’s plans for new reciprocal tariffs on U.S. imports led to concerns of rising inflation. All told, the overall U.S. bond market, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index, returned 2.78% during the first quarter. In contrast, the municipal (“muni”) market modestly declined over the quarter.
  • Contributors: Duration and yield curve positioning, along with spread sector exposures, contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: Hedging costs were a modest detractor from performance.
  • Outlook: We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical.

Performance Review

  • Duration exposure was the largest contributor to performance as rates declined over the quarter.
  • Yield curve positioning was additive for results.
  • The Fund’s structured product exposure, including non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities, contributed to returns.
  • An allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies positively impacted performance as the U.S. dollar weakened over the period.
  • An allocation to emerging markets was rewarded.
  • Hedging costs were a modest detractor from performance.

Outlook

  • The disinflationary trend may be interrupted as tariffs and retaliatory actions are implemented, but we expect inflation to move lower again over the longer term. Monetary policy remains restrictive. We expect central banks will continue to cut rates modestly in 2025. The Fed remains well positioned to provide support if the U.S. economy falters. Public debt levels continue to rise and yield curves may steepen given concerns over fiscal policies globally.
  • We see pockets of opportunity in developed market rates in Europe, the UK and Australia. While the overall uncertainty in the market environment necessitates caution, we do see some longer-term value opportunities in emerging market local currency debt.
  • Spread sector fundamentals remain supportive, but valuations reflect those fundamentals and credit spreads persist at below historical averages. We continue to find opportunities within spread sectors and related securities, while remaining tactical.
  • Developed market duration provides useful diversification.

Benchmark 3=ICE BofA US Dollar 3-Month Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit Mutual Funds | ETFs | Insights.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns. Net expenses reflect contractual fee waivers, expense caps and/or reimbursements, which cannot be terminated prior to 12/31/2025 without Board consent. Additional amounts may be voluntarily waived and/or reimbursed and may be modified or discontinued at any time without notice.

Maximum sales charges have been updated as of August 15, 2022, please refer the Fund’s prospectus for more information.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. Asset-backed, mortgage-backed or mortgage-related securities are subject to prepayment and extension risks. Low-rated, high-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. International investments are subject to special risks, including currency fluctuations and social, economic and political uncertainties, which could increase volatility. These risks are magnified in emerging markets. Derivative instruments can be illiquid, may disproportionately increase losses, and have a potentially large impact on performance. Leverage increases the volatility of investment returns and subjects investments to magnified losses and a decline in value. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Commercial mortgage-backed securities are a type of mortgage-backed security backed by commercial mortgages rather than residential real estate. CMBS tend to be more complex and volatile than residential mortgage-backed securities due to the unique nature of the underlying property assets.

Residential mortgage-backed securities are a type of mortgage-backed debt obligation created from residential debt, such as mortgages, home-equity loans and subprime mortgages.

The yield curve shows the relationship between yields and maturity dates for a similar class of bonds.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The Bloomberg US Aggregate Index is comprised of investment-grade, U.S. dollar-denominated government, corporate, and mortgage- and asset-backed issues having at least one year to maturity. Source: Bloomberg Indices. The ICE BofA 3-Month U.S. Treasury Bill Index is an unmanaged index that comprises a single U.S. Treasury issue with approximately three months to final maturity, purchased at the beginning of each month and held for one full month. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use. The ICE BofA US Dollar 3-Month Deposit Offered Rate Constant Maturity Index tracks the performance of a synthetic asset paying Libor to a stated maturity. The index is based on the assumed purchase at par of a synthetic instrument having exactly its stated maturity and with a coupon equal to that day’s fixing rate. That issue is assumed to be sold the following business day (priced at a yield equal to the current day fixing rate) and rolled into a new instrument. Source: The index data referenced herein is the property of Intercontinental Exchange (“ICE”) and/or its licensors and has been licensed for use by Franklin Templeton. ICE and its licensors accept no liability in connection with this use.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Effective Duration is a duration calculation for bonds with embedded options. Effective duration takes into account that expected cash flows will fluctuate as interest rates change. Duration measures the sensitivity of price (the value of principal) of a fixed-income investment to a change in interest rates. The higher the duration number, the more sensitive a fixed-income investment will be to interest rate changes. The 30-day SEC yield is calculated using the net income (interest and dividends) per share earned over a trailing 30-day period (annualized), divided by the fund’s share price at the end of that period. It may not equal the fund’s actual income distribution rate, which reflects the fund’s past dividends paid to shareholders.

Credit Quality is a measure of a bond issuer’s ability to repay interest and principal in a timely manner. The credit ratings shown are based on each portfolio security’s rating as provided by Standard and Poor’s, Moody’s Investors Service and/or Fitch Ratings, Ltd. and typically range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest), or an equivalent and/or similar rating. For this purpose, if two or more of the agencies have assigned differing ratings to a security, the highest rating is used. Securities that are unrated by all three agencies are reflected as such. The credit quality of the investments in the fund’s portfolio does not apply to the stability or safety of the fund. These ratings are updated monthly and may change over time. Please note, the Fund itself has not been rated by an independent rating agency.

© Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

Western Asset Management Company, LLC, and Franklin Distributors, LLC, are Franklin Templeton companies.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bain Capital Specialty Finance: Lower Earnings But Fundamentals & Dividend Coverage Are Strong
data.description:
Overview

Business Development Companies continue to be one of my favorite sectors to utilize as a way to take advantage of the higher interest rate environment. However, a prolonged period of unfavorable market conditions has proven that all BDCs aren't constructed the same and there are a ton of variables that can influence the overall performance. When I previously covered Bain Capital Specialty Lending (NYSE:BCSF), I issued a buy rating due to the strong underlying fundamentals related to its earnings, low non-accruals, and positive NAV momentum. The BDC recently reported its Q1 earnings so I wanted to use this as an opportunity to provide some updated insights.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that BCSF's price has decreased by nearly 8%. However, most of this decline has taken place the last two months as President Trump's announced new trade policies related to tariffs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders during the same time frame, the total return remains positive at approximately 2%. One of the most attractive aspects to BCSF is the generous starting dividend yield of about 11.4%. This double-digit yield makes it an enticing choice for investors looking to create a supplemental income stream with dividends.

Following the recent price decline BCSF now trades at a discount to NAV. I believe this remains an attractive time for accumulation as the underlying fundamentals of the BDC remain strong. Management continues to prioritize allocating capital towards new investment activity and non-accruals are relatively low. The latest earnings report indicates that BCSF can remain resilient through challenging market conditions, which leaves me to believe that patient long term investors can be rewarded for their patience. So let's start by taking a quick look at the underlying strategy and structure of BCSF's portfolio.

Fund Strategy

According to BCSF's latest portfolio breakdown, their investments have a fair value of $2.46B, which is a slight year over year increase. Additionally, these investments are spread across 175 different positions at a weighted average yield of 11.5%. The amount of portfolio companies increased year over year, but the weighted average yield was reduced.

Additionally, 93.2% of their portfolio of debt investments are structured on a floating rate basis. In theory, this should allow BCSF to capitalize on the current interest rate environment. A higher interest rate should translate to a higher amount of interest income that it can collect from borrowers.

Part of BCSF's strategy is to maintain a diverse range of exposure to a variety of industries. This reduces BCSF's vulnerability to any one specific sector that may be going through headwinds. It's worth mentioning that the combination of the top three sectors makes up approximately 37% of the portfolio, so we can assume that these will be the largest drivers of price growth going forward. Since we don't necessary know what sort of ripple effects higher tariffs will have on the underlying portfolio, this level of diversity can be helpful at mitigating any negative impacts. The breakdown on the allocation of assets within BCSF is as follows:

  • Investment Vehicles - 16%.
    • Includes an International Senior Loan Program, as well as a Senior Loan Program.
  • Aerospace & Defense - 11%
  • Services: Business- 10%
  • High Tech Industries - 8%
  • Healthcare & Pharmaceuticals - 7%
  • Transportation: Cargo - 5%
  • Automotive - 5%
  • Consumer Goods - 5%
  • Telecoms - 4%
  • Other - 29%

Additionally, 64% of the debt investments are structured on a first lien basis. First lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. This structure is the most ideal and can be interpreted as a defensive feature of BCSF since it reduces the probability of BCSF losing all invested capital in a scenario where a borrower needs to liquidate assets in order to paydown debt obligations.

Q1 Earnings & Risk Profile

BCSF recently reported their Q1 earnings and I believe the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.50 per share, beating expectations by $0.03 per share. However total investment income experienced a year over year decrease from $74.5M down to $66.8M. This can be attributed to a lower amount of interest income and dividend income received. To offset this, BCSF was able to reduce its expenses down from $39.5M to $33.6M. This ultimately led to net investment income of $32.1M, which was only a slightly reduction from the prior year's net investment income of $33.9M.

The portfolio did experience net realized losses of $23.9M. Despite this, BCSF still managed to see a positive net increase in assets of $28.5M, which is what we like to see. BCSF has been able to keep its NAV steady over time but I would like to see some growth take place when conditions improve. Just for reference, here are the NAV amounts for each of the last few quarters:

  • Q1 2024: $17.70 per share
  • Q2 2024: $17.70 per share
  • Q3 2024: $17.76 per share
  • Q4 2024: $17.65 per share
  • Q1 2025: $17.64 per share

I plan to continue monitoring the NAV throughout each quarter since a BDC's NAV growth is one of the clearest indications on whether or not the underlying strategy is effective. I believe that larger strides need to be made in order to help boost the NAV. For instance, BCSF was able to allocate $277.2M towards new investment activity during the quarter. $140.3M of this capital went towards 13 new portfolio companies while another $134.4M went towards 75 existing portfolio companies. However, sales and repayments for the quarter amounted to $246.4M, which ultimately landed at a net investment activity of only $30.8M. For reference, BCSF's net investment activity for Q1 of 2024 was much larger at $107.1M.

In terms of the risk profile, I like to measure the shifts in non-accrual rates every quarter. Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. Therefore, borrowers on non-accrual status aren't actively contributing to BCSF's earnings and they drag down the overall quality of the rest of the portfolio. It does seem like non-accruals have slightly increased from the time of my last coverage, but only slightly.

As of the latest earnings call, non-accruals now amount to 1.4% of cost and 0.7% of fair portfolio value. This is a very slight increase from the previously non-accrual rate of 1.3% of cost and 0.2% of fair value. Despite the slight increase, I don't think this raises any red alarms yet since it's quite common to see non-accruals increase for many other high quality BDCs. Just for reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs that also reported earnings recently.

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.8% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Golub Capital (GBDC): 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Even though non-accruals are only one piece of the pie, we can see how BCSF's performance is on par with these peers.

Updated Valuation

When I previously covered BCSF, the price traded at a small premium to NAV valuation of about 8.05%. However, the recent price declines have caused BCSF to now trade at a more favorable discount to NAV valuation. BCSF now trades at a sizeable discount to NAV of 13.6%, which is pretty close to BCSF's three year average discount to NAV of about 14.2%. After covering a ton of BDCs over the years, I've noticed a clear trend: higher quality BDCs consistently trade at a premium while lower quality BDCs trade in the discount territory.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $16.67 per share. This indicates a potential upside of about 8.7% from the current level. I do think this is very attainable for BCSF if macro conditions improve and they improve the net investment activity. The primary challenge that BCSF may be facing is the limited amount of new deals in the market while interest rates remain elevated. Higher interest rates mean that the cost of taking on new debt is more costly, which can slow the pace of new businesses seeking capital.

Since the majority of BCSF's portfolio is structured on a floating rate basis, higher interest rates also have an effect on existing portfolio companies. Higher interest rates means that existing borrowers need to pay higher interest expenses on their debt. This can negatively impact margins and make it more difficult for their earnings to grow. Despite the Fed estimating slower economic growth, they still estimate that there can be two smaller interest rate cuts of 25-basis-points at some point through 2025.

Interest rate cuts can actually serve as a catalyst for growth. Since the cost of debt is lower, it could cause an influx of new borrowers seeking capital that BCSF can capitalize on by increasing their new investment activity. Even if the interest rate cuts aren't sizeable, they can shift markets to have a more optimistic stance on BDCs.

Strong Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.42 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.4%. Management also declared a supplemental distribution of $0.03 per share for the quarter, through December of 2025. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $0.50 per share. This would indicate that BCSF's earnings support the distribution at a rate of 111%, including the supplemental. If we exclude the supplemental payout, the base dividend coverage sits at 119%.

Looking over BCSF's history, we can see that the dividend payout has steadily increased after getting reduced back in 2020. With a comfortable level of coverage at the moment, I wouldn't expect any base raises but I do feel confident that the current payouts can be sustained throughout the remainder of the year. Plus, BCSF's liquidity is strong with $699M in undrawn credit facility and another $94M in cash and equivalents. Lastly, BCSF has spillover income of $1.41 per share, which represents a 3x coverage of their base quarterly distribution amount.

With these strong coverage metrics in mind, I believe that patient investors can be rewarded by accumulating here. Once macro conditions improve, I have confidence that BCSF will be able to efficiently capitalize and boost earnings. However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from BCSF are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the least favorable tax consequences, so a position may be best suited within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on BCSF as the underlying fundamentals remain solid, the distribution remains well supported, and the portfolio quality has avoided any serious deterioration. While there are certainly places that BCSF can improve, I believe weaknesses related to NAV growth and pricing can be attributed to macro-economic conditions, rather than to any sort of internal weakness of BCSF's strategy. Future interest rate cuts can serve as a catalyst so patient investors that are willing to remain invested can be rewarded. In the meantime, I am confident that the current distribution can be sustained throughout the remainder of 2025.

data.title: CDL: A Defensive, 3.3%-Yielding Complement To Other Dividend ETFs
data.description:
The VictoryShares US Large Cap High Dividend Volatility-Weighted ETF (NASDAQ:CDL) has a long and rather convoluted name, but its strategy is fairly simple and straightforward.

It combines large market capitalization, relatively high dividend yields, and lower volatility into a 100-stock basket. The end result is an ETF with a fairly high dividend yield as well as robust and consistent dividend growth, even if the ETF falls short of its goal to generate lower-than-average overall volatility.

In my view, CDL makes a good, defensive complement to other dividend ETFs and can play a valuable role in diversifying an investment portfolio that's focused on income generation and dividend growth.

Let's take a look at how CDL's stock basket is constructed and then compare it to several of its close peers in the dividend ETF space.

How CDL's Portfolio Is Constructed

Most ETFs' stock portfolios are weighted by market cap. That's standard practice. Some dividend ETFs choose to weight by dividend yield, or a combination of yield and market cap.

What makes CDL unique is that after it has narrowed down its investable universe by profitability and market cap, it then weights its 100 holdings by volatility. The least volatile stocks are given the largest weight.

All stocks in the investable universe are US-based. It must be one of the largest 500 stocks by market cap, excluding REITs. Within that 500-stock investable universe, the system then picks the 100 highest yielding stocks. Finally, that list of the 100 highest yielding stocks are weighted by volatility.

The index is reconstituted twice a year, in March and September.

And voila! You have CDL's portfolio.

CDL As A Complement To Other Dividend ETFs

Let's do a quick comparison between CDL and several other popular high dividend ETFs. For the comparison, I'll only look at ETFs with a relatively high yield of 2.7% or higher. (If you think that's a low yield, take a look at dividend growth ETFs.)

Here are the four I'll compare CDL to:

  • Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)
  • Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM)
  • iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV)
  • WisdomTree US High Dividend ETF (DHS)

Firstly, we should address what isn't a good reason to own CDL, and it pertains to volatility.

If you're expecting CDL to exhibit lower volatility or less downside during market downturns than other dividend ETFs, you're out of luck. While CDL's holdings are weighted based on the lowness of their volatility, the whole ETF isn't actually less volatile than most of its peers.

Here's a look at these ETFs' beta (volatility relative to the market) over the last decade:

CDL's beta in the 0.6x territory is fairly low, but it isn't the lowest. Other ETFs that don't target low-volatility happen to have similarly low levels of overall volatility.

And if we look at the average drawdown from record highs for each of these five ETFs, we find that CDL's average drawdown is on the higher end of the range for its peer group:

This indicates that CDL does not have any more downside protection than its peers.

For example, during the COVID-19 selloff in early 2020, CDL had one of the largest peak-to-trough declines.

Second, let's look at assets under management ("AUM") to show the relative size of each ETF.

Assets Under Management
CDL $333 million
SCHD $66.35 billion
VYM $70.42 billion
HDV $10.96 billion
DHS $1.19 billion

As you can see, CDL is by far the smallest ETF by AUM. It's also the youngest ETF measured by inception date.

To be fair, though, since the beginning of 2016, CDL has enjoyed the second-most AUM growth of this group:

On the other hand, you can probably see in this chart above that CDL's AUM has stagnated over the last few years.

Zooming in to the last three-year period, we find that CDL's AUM growth has been a lot more modest, basically in line with that of VYM, while SCHD has captured the vast majority of investor market share:

One thing that might be holding back CDL from growing its AUM faster is its expense ratio.

Note that CDL's expense ratio is on the higher end of its peer group:

Expense Ratio
CDL 0.35%
SCHD 0.06%
VYM 0.06%
HDV 0.08%
DHS 0.38%

Only DHS has a higher expense ratio. This relatively high expense ratio is undeniably a ding against both DHS and CDL, as it will incrementally eat into returns over long periods of time.

Speaking of returns, let's compare these ETFs' total returns over the last decade or so since CDL's inception in October 2015.

Ostensibly, these are all moderately high-yielding dividend stock ETFs. But there are significant differences in their strategies and stock-picking methodologies, as illustrated by the variance in their total return performance over the last (almost) decade:

Since October 2015, SCHD has generated the highest total returns, followed by CDL and closely thereafter by VYM.

It is impressive to me that CDL and VYM both turned in strong performance in recent years despite stagnant AUM growth.

If we zoom in to the post-COVID recovery period since the middle of 2021, the leadership gets reshuffled. Now DHS and HDV, two of the laggards over the last 10-year period, are in the lead, while SCHD has been lagging behind.

This SCHD underperformance in recent years is a strange reversal of its very strong performance up through 2021. My guess would be that SCHD has proven to be more vulnerable to rising interest rates than its dividend ETF peers.

More recently, the ETF's annual reconstitution in March turned out to be unfortunately timed, as it added several oil producers based on backwards-looking data right before the price of oil began to drop.

Interestingly, over the last year, DHS has turned in the best performance of this peer group:

How has that been the case? I think it's due to DHS's particular stock selection.

Let's compare the top five sectors of each ETF to illustrate the differences in their portfolios:

Largest Sector 2nd Largest 3rd Largest 4th Largest 5th Largest
CDL Utilities (26%) Cons. Defensive (23%) Financials (17%) Energy (11%) Healthcare (8%)
SCHD Cons. Defensive (20%) Energy (19%) Healthcare (15%) Industrials (11%) Technology (10%)
VYM Financials (21%) Healthcare (15%) Technology (13%) Cons. Defensive (13%) Industrials (11%)
HDV Cons. Defensive (22%) Energy (20%) Healthcare (17%) Financials (13%) Utilities (10%)
DHS Cons. Defensive (21%) Healthcare (19%) Financials (19%) Energy (12%) Utilities (10%)

Over the last twelve months, financials and consumer staples have outperformed, while energy and technology have underperformed.

DHS has the largest share of its portfolio (40%) concentrated in financials and consumer staples, with among the lowest shares of its portfolio in energy and tech (~16%).

CDL, which has turned in the second best total return performance over the last year, has even less of its portfolio in energy and tech (~14%) with about the same share of financials and consumer staples (40%).

What I particularly like about CDL is its heavy concentration in the most defensive sectors of the economy -- utilities and staples, which together account for about half its portfolio. This is a result of the ETF's volatility weighting system.

Regulated utilities are not only defensive bond proxies but also a steady source of moderate growth in the age of AI and increasing electricity usage.

Of course, the utilities sector currently averages a forward P/E multiple of 18.5x, which strikes me as being fully valued. But from a fundamental basis, utilities have very little downside risk in almost any conceivable macroeconomic scenario. Thus, while the sector isn't undervalued right now, in my estimation, it is reasonably priced given its defensiveness and all-weather growth profile.

Utilities typically make up only a small portion of other dividend ETFs. SCHD holds zero utilities. VYM has 6% exposure. And both HDV and DHS have only 10% exposure.

If for no other reason than the large utilities weighting, I believe CDL makes a good complement to other dividend ETFs.

Finally, let's compare the dividend growth of all five dividend ETFs from 2016 to the last 12 months:

2016 Dividends Q1 2025 LTM Dividends Growth
CDL $1.19 $2.10 76.5%
SCHD $0.42 $1.03 145.2%
VYM $2.21 $3.68 66.5%
HDV $2.71 $4.08 50.6%
DHS $2.21 $3.45 56.1%

What has made SCHD so popular over the last decade is arguably its rare combination of a relatively high yield and very strong dividend growth. Whether such high ETF payout growth can continue into the future is up for debate, but I'll point out that CDL also boasts quite an attractive dividend profile.

Between CDL's 3.3% dividend yield and its consistent mid- to high-single-digit annual dividend growth, the ETF achieves a better overall profile for dividend investing purposes than most other dividend ETFs.

Bottom Line

In summary, I find two reasons not to like CDL and several other reasons to like it.

The first reason not to like CDL is its above-average expense ratio of 0.35%. The second reason not to like CDL is that it doesn't actually have a record of trading at lower volatility than its dividend ETF peers.

Now, if you can live with that, I think there are several reasons to like CDL as an investment for income and dividend growth investors.

  1. It has a strong track record of total returns since its inception.
  2. It boasts a robust record of dividend growth over the last decade.
  3. The roughly half of its portfolio in the utilities and consumer staples sectors makes it arguably the most fundamentally defensive dividend ETF in the peer group above.

I recently took a small starter position in CDL because I believe it should be able to navigate the current tariff-induced economic malaise better (at least fundamentally) than the average dividend ETF.

The main appeal to a long-term Dividend Growth Investor like me is its combination of defensiveness and dividend growth.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: It Is Likely Trading A Tad Rich
data.description:
Deutsche Bank Aktiengesellschaft's (NYSE:DB) ADRs have enjoyed a stunning run in recent quarters, producing over 60% in total returns. We covered the bank on numerous occasions in the past year, primarily aligning our sentiment with a positive outlook. Today's article re-engages with Deutsche, whereby we share our latest thoughts with close consideration paid to the bank's recent earnings report and the systematic credit environment.

Without further ado, herewith are our latest thoughts.

Operating Review

Deutsche Bank released its first-quarter earnings in April, revealing a pre-tax profit of €2.8 Billion (+39% year-over-year). Among the catalysts were private banking, which recorded €490 Million in quarterly pre-tax profit (+43% year-over-year), asset management, €204 Million (+67% year-over-year), and investment banking, €1.5 Billion (+22% year-over-year). On the other end of the spectrum, Deutsche's commercial banking segment's pre-tax profit grew by a modest 3% year-over-year, settling at €632 Million.

A summation of the bank's segmental results is presented below, a discussion follows.

Much of Deutsche's recent progression derived from cost-cutting and market share growth efforts. For example, during Q1, Deutsche's private banking unit closed an additional 60 branches and downsized full-time employee headcount by 400 while attracting nearly €6 Billion in net inflows, showing resilient strategic execution.

Cost reduction isn't the only driving force behind Deutsche's performance. The bank's asset management segment has experienced significant inflows in recent quarters, bolstering the division's management fees to €490 Million in Q1, an 8% year-over-year increase.

As previously mentioned, Deutsche's investment banking results improved in Q1. Most of the growth derived from fixed income, currency, and commodity services (+17%), overshadowing a slump in the bank's advisory and origination offerings, which receded by 8% year-over-year. At face value, the results suggest reliance on various currency derivative settlement, hedging, and fixed income repositioning instead of IPOs and M&A, which can be a differentiator in today's uncertain equity market environment.

Looking Ahead

Asset Management and Private Banking To Remain Steady

In our view, asset management and private banking are two long-term orientated segments. The latter is on a cost-cutting and optimisation spree, which will likely see it improve its throughout-the-cycle profits, especially if its market share growth remains intact. Furthermore, asset management's AUM growth hasn't shown signs of slowing down. While some asset managers might struggle to maintain AUM in the slowing economic environment, we think Deutsche will leverage cross-selling and market share to maintain AUM growth throughout-the-cycle.

NII Might Succumb To Systematic Risk

Deutsche's net interest income, also known as NII, has shown signs of pivoting. NII has remained steady but certain segmental margins are flatlining. We foresee a challenging NII environment occurring in late 2025/early 2026; an explanation of the claim follows the next diagram.

Among the primary NII headwinds are slowing deposits and loan commitments. Data shows that Deutsche has pulled back on quarterly loan commitments amid a slowdown in corporate and private banking deposits. The rise in geopolitical tensions and the uncertainty embedded in EU zone economic growth will likely see banks, including Deutsche, temper their lending volume. In addition, fears of lower deposit volumes might settle in given that EU zone short-term interest rates have fallen (savings accounts have become less competitive), adding a secondary layer of risk.

Furthermore, global yield curves are volatile. The U.S. and certain EU curves are pent up amid fears of a tariff-driven inflationary environment. However, as explained in one of our previous articles, we think a loss in trade confidence, lower consumer sentiment, and uncertain business confidence will lead global curves lower in late 2025 and beyond. As shown by Deutsche's sensitivity analysis, downward curve levelling might dampen NII, despite benefits arising from lower funding costs.

To conclude this subsection, I'd like to highlight Deutsche deciison to set aside funds to cover the geopolitically-linked uptick in credit risk. According to the bank's CEO, Christian Sewing: "The geopolitical landscape is rapidly evolving and uncertainty and volatility are likely to stay elevated for the time being."

The above-mentioned likely means Deutsche foresees higher counterparty and collateral value risks emerging, which is consistent with the recent spikes in U.S. credit spreads. Consequently, we foresee provisions settling higher in due course. Alternatively, we think assets might be marked down due to credit adjustments.

Predicting Moderate IB Growth

We don't see much potential in the global equity and debt issuance space nor do we see activity spiking in M&A anytime soon. Our basis is largely formed on market uncertainty and slowing business confidence. That said, Deutsche's investment banking has benefitted from FX and fixed income offerings lately. Unlike issuances and advisory, we see ex-commodity FICC growing at a modest rate as currency volatility and migration of sovereign bond allocation (out of the U.S. and into EU Zone) is prominent. While we don't think a blowout fiscal 2025 will occur, we acknowledge the division's potential ex-commodity FICC-driven growth prospects.

Valuation and Dividends

Baseline Metrics

A glance at valuation shows that Deutsche's ADRs still trade below book value per unit. However, context must be added: Deustche's five-year median price-to-book ratio is far below 1x. In fact, the current ADR price-to-book ratio of approximately 0.6x is materially above its five-year median of 0.33x. Although Deutsche's return on equity of 5.01% remains above its five-year median, convergence has occurred. Considering these two variables, we think it would be wise if market participants revised Deutsche's valuation outlook.

As for dividends, Deutsche's recent rally depleted its dividend yield to roughly 1.83%. Though some might disagree, we think the yield provides minimal benefit, unless a rapid dividend growth trajectory unfolds.

Shareholder Compensation Plan

For those interested, here's a look at Deutsche's shareholder compensation plan (see diagram below). The plan shows a commitment to shareholders while maintaining enough capital to reinvest in growth initiatives. The clearly devised plan may stimulate investor confidence but we ultimately see systematic valuation drivers predominating.

Conclusion

We previously covered Deutsche in January, maintaining our bullish outlook on the premise of fundamental strength and valuation benefits. The bank has since shown additional strengths, including its fixed income and currency offerings, private banking market share growth, and enterprise-wide cost reductions.

However, despite Deutsche's ongoing strenghts, systematic risk is heating up, posing threats to the bank's net interest income and investment banking advisory services. In addition, Deutsche is trading above its five-year price-to-book median and arguably possesses a compressed dividend yield.

After considering a host of factors, we decided to downgrade our opinion of Deutsche to 'Hold.'

data.title: Hold Fast To This Amazing 9% Yield: Ares Capital
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

"A calm sea doesn't make a skilled sailor."

– Franklin D. Roosevelt

Avast has permeated our society in reference to speaking like a pirate or when engaging media filled with pirates or a maritime focus. Yet, avast is an alteration from houd vast, the Dutch command to hold fast.

The original command was to advise the sailors on the ship to hold tight to the ship, the riggings, or ropes so that they would not be swept off to sea. You see, a calm sea rarely makes for a skilled sailor because if you've never been tried or tested, you don't have the experience that is necessary to become a skilled sailor, one who has faced the dangers of the sea and come out the other side successfully. As the term morphed with time, it became a way to tell people to stop to and hold still, and thus avast became a much more common way to tell your crew, if you were the captain, to stop what they were doing and listen; thus we get the saying avast, me hearties.

When it comes to the market, I like to have holdings in my portfolio that provide me with outstanding income regardless of the situation going on outside. In essence, I want skilled and weathered sailors who can help me drive my income vessel through the hardest of the storms, whether that's a bear market, a recession, a technical correction, or a bull market where the sun is shining and there is calmness in the sea. I want them to be able to perform their roles expertly and deftly, regardless of what's going on all around.

Today, we're going to look at a company we have reviewed before and see how well it's performing in this most recent quarter by reviewing its earnings.

Let's dive in!

Hold Fast To Stable Income

"Stable" is probably the best adjective to use to describe Ares Capital's (NASDAQ:ARCC) Q1 2025 earnings. ARCC, yielding 9.3%, is the largest publicly traded BDC (Business Development Company) which provides essential liquidity and management assistance to the usually hard-to-access middle market. Investors can buy shares of a BDC to gain valuable exposure and income from this usually privately held sector of the American economy.

ARCC saw NAV decline 0.4% to $19.82, a normal gyration, and Net Investment Income came in at $0.54, a penny below Q4 2024. Source

ARCC made $3.4 billion in new gross commitments, which is lower than Q4 2024 but close to a year ago. ARCC's leverage ticked down to 0.98x debt/equity, from 0.99x at the end of Q4.

The one metric that was materially different was Core EPS, which removes the impact of realized capital gains/losses and the incentive fee. At $0.50/share, it comfortably covers the $0.48 dividend, but is lower than what we saw last year, primarily due to lower average interest rates.

As we've discussed over the past several years, BDCs tend to capture a windfall when interest rates are rising as they tend to use long-term fixed-rate debt for their liabilities, while the debt they invest in is primarily floating-rate.

One thing we've seen ARCC do is start taking out interest rate swaps on their unsecured bonds. Approximately $7.1 billion of ARCC's debt is effectively floating, which includes just over $3.7 billion in fixed-rate unsecured bonds that have interest rate swaps in place.

These swaps were placed on debt that was taken out after interest rates went up, with coupons ranging from 5.8%-6.9%. Converting this debt to variable through interest expense means paying a little more right now, but if SOFR comes down, it could mean paying a lot less in the future. It means that ARCC's bottom line will be less impacted by declining rates and will provide more earnings stability going forward.

ARCC's loans on non-accrual ticked down last quarter and are now 1.5% of amortized cost-basis and 0.9% of fair value:

ARCC's default rate has varied from 1.3% to 1.7% over the past year. For comparison, this number was over 3% in 2021. While higher interest rates initially put strain on ARCC's portfolio companies, they have adapted to the new normal, and credit quality remains strong.

BDCs like ARCC take on credit risk. In other words, they are lending money, and the risk of lending always comes down to whether or not the borrower will repay as agreed. One of the major concerns about a recession for a BDC like ARCC is that more borrowers will default, and they will have more realized losses. Worse, a spike in defaults leads to capital being tied up in non-accruals where it isn't earning cash flow for ARCC to use for things like dividends.

ARCC has a great track record in navigating challenging times, being one of a handful of publicly traded BDCs that went through the GFC, and came out the other side stronger than ever. Beyond that, it is worth noting that while the economy is getting softer in many ways, we have not seen the high levels of borrowing from both customers and lenders that we have seen in previous credit cycles. Going into the GFC, borrowers were leveraged to the hilt, and so were the lenders. When the borrowers failed, the banks did as well because they were overleveraged and couldn't sustain the level of defaults.

Today, we are in a very different macro-environment. ARCC is operating below 1.0x debt/equity, while maintaining a very high level of liquidity. If borrowers do start running into issues, ARCC has the flexibility in its balance sheet to pursue a variety of options.

Yet borrowers are posting solid credit metrics themselves, with an average leverage of 5.7x debt/EBITDA, down from 5.9x a year ago. EBITDA interest coverage is 1.9x, up from 1.6x a year ago:

There is always a risk of credit metrics deteriorating, but these levels point toward a mild credit cycle. Whether it was COVID, inflation, or rapidly rising interest rates, borrowers have taken a more conservative approach to managing their balance sheets than we have seen in historical credit cycles. So as tariffs, economic slowdowns, or even a recession impact borrowers, we will certainly see some rise in defaults. It's hard to get below 1.5% non-accruals for a meaningful length of time, but it is a good bet they will be lower than we saw during a credit crisis like the GFC. If we are wrong, ARCC has the stability and strength we want in our portfolio to be confident about weathering a crisis.

Conclusion

Whenever I talk to somebody about how income investing does not mean that you have to sacrifice outstanding returns, they scoff. It's not uncommon to run into commenters who have not been exposed to or educated about income investments, who think that investing for income means accepting lower returns all the time. Looking over the lifespan of ARCC versus a very common market-wide passive ETF (SPY)(SP500), we can see ARCC, through its lifetime, has vastly outperformed all while spitting out wonderful income:

If you're a "total return" investor, ARCC should be in your portfolio, yet many overlook it because it's an "income investment!". This common misconception shows the inexperience of many investors versus the skilled experience investors in the market. Like a sailor who runs away from a storm, many total return investors ignore income investments because they don't choose to learn about them or understand them, or wrongly assume that they are all underperformers. A seasoned, experienced investor will do the research in the diligence to understand further.

Income investing is not easily done by the inexperienced, largely because so many income investments can be bad, yields can be deceiving, and further research is always required. This is why we try to provide a base-level education to every single investor who joins High Dividend Opportunities. We've become Seeking Alpha's single largest investment community because we want as many people as possible to learn and grow on their journey. We provide public content as a service towards that as well.

ARCC belongs in a retirement portfolio, as a skilled sailor who can help you generate massive income regardless of the storms raging in the world around you. They can be a great first mate, helping you generate great outperformance and generous levels of income.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Ticks All Boxes But Caution Ahead
data.description:
Blue Owl Capital Corporation (NYSE:OBDC), a business development company, managed under the bigger umbrella of Blue Owl, is an interesting investment case with decent return on equity (~10.2%) reported in Q1 2025 earnings at the time of writing. New commitments were reported at ~$1.2b, down from $1.7b reported last quarter. Minor decreases in NAV and NII were reported. Total investment income increased ~16% YoY. Mixed signals, some expected as the economy braces for a downturn (NII down, NAV down and rising non accruals) but otherwise a good start to the year (merger effects with Blue Owl and high RoE).

Positioning and Prospects

OBDC benefits from the Blue Owl umbrella in terms of credibility and client relationships. It provides senior secured loans to upper middle market companies, an area banks are increasingly retreating from. I also expect tighter regulations making new competition entry very difficult. As such, OBDC's positioning is strong - a material presence in a less competitive space with barriers to entry. When I say less competitive, I am acknowledging competition from other BDCs like Ares Capital and Blackstone Credit, but mostly minus banks.

BDCs face less regulatory drag than banks. The operational flexibility is also enhanced due to better access to liquidity than banks. BDCs raise capital from private equity and long-term debt and are more resilient to volatile market scenarios than the banks' reliance on deposits as capital.

On the downside, though, the same flexibility translates to lower asset quality and risks that arise thereof.

Analyzing OBDC's Portfolio

The fair value of the portfolio reported in Q1 2025 was ~$17.6, aided by the merger with Blue Owl III (from $13.1b last quarter). This still lags behind some of the bigger BDCs like Ares Capital but commands sufficient size in the BDC world to get access to high-quality deals. The size also allows for significant diversification, unlike a small BDC would. More concentration increases the risk of potential defaults even in small pockets. OBDC has 236 portfolio companies to diversify its exposure. We do not know whether the top exposures are significantly weighing on its books, but the numbers are comforting at first look. I also like the >75% exposure to first lien debt investments. OBDC also has investment grade credit ratings of BBB-/BBB (from Fitch and S&P).

Dividends and NII

The dividends (including special dividends) of the past few quarters have been very predictable and well cushioned by the NII per share. The coverage ratio shows that OBDC will have strength to support dividends for a couple of stressed quarters without having to dig into borrowings or asset sale. That is good news for the income investor. Without the special dividend that was paid out in the past quarters ($0.01 in Q1 2025), the current yield is at ~10%. That drop may continue if the special dividend is stopped under stress, but still looks steady and lucrative income. Interestingly, during the last duress of the 2020 COVID downturn too, OBDC was paying special dividends (although irregular). Recession depth of any upcoming downturn may challenge the income, but historical signs are good.

Tailwinds from Rates

As ~96% of the exposure is in floating rate lending, a period of plateauing or falling rates will mean asset yield spreads will compress. This will impact the NII. All the argument of historical stability of NII and dividends fall apart in this environment when we acknowledge OBDC's lack of operating data and experience in a falling rates cycle. Some comfort can be derived from the fact that the partial roll back in rates so far has not impacted OBDC significantly. In fact, non-accruals as a percentage of the portfolio was an extremely tolerable 0.4% in Q4 2024 and down from 0.6% seen in Q3 2024. This number has risen to 0.8% in Q1 2025, which is a worrying trend, but expected as the credit stress starts to take shape, but not significant in an absolute sense.

Tailwinds from Delinquencies

Concerns and signs of credit cycle distress are high. According to some dated observations from the Fed and Moody's, delinquency rates for leveraged loans are on the rise and are likely to be magnified further, especially for lower rated credit. Recession fears, mingled with a tariff environment that could impact rates, make it a difficult to predict 2025 and 2026 for the credit markets. The situation becomes trickier if the Fed rates remain high in a recession, pressuring repayment. Lower rates compress margins. Although the delinquencies' situation till Q1 2025 is still not heavily evident, credit stress starts appearing quite late into the stress cycle. So, the current apparent strength in the underwriting discipline looks good, but stress reactions should be still considered unknown.

How Do The Financials Look

The debt to equity leverage ratio is comfortable at 1.26x as of Q1 2025, indicating conservative leverage exposure (but again rising QoQ), even in comparison with what BDCs typically can do.

The cash position does not look particularly concerning for shock scenarios. As per the Q1 2025 report

As of March 31, 2025, the Company had $514.2 million in cash and restricted cash, $10.3 billion in total principal value of debt outstanding, including $2.5 billion of undrawn capacity(1) on the Company's credit facilities and $5.2 billion of unsecured notes. The funding mix was composed of 50% secured and 50% unsecured borrowings as of March 31, 2025 on an outstanding basis.

However, in extreme crisis scenarios, there are concerns about liquidity as the sizeable debt is larger than cash reserves. Generating free cash flow would also be a challenge in such a scenario to meet debt obligations.

Investment Action on OBDC

The price to NAV (basis reported NAV in Q1 2024 of $15.14 per share) ratio currently stands at ~0.9x. The discount of 10% is good, but is a reflection of the higher perceived risks, particularly in the current environment due to interest rate volatility and credit cycle risks, as discussed earlier. Discounts can go down further if dividends shrink or asset quality goes down.

The plunge in share prices around the tariff sell off opened up this 10% discount, which was not available earlier. OBDC has a strong performance history (though untested in stress cycles) and positions itself as a relatively conservative BDC in terms of leverage and portfolio quality. In the face of upcoming crisis, and some cracks on NAV, NII and non-accruals visible this quarter, the "Buy" thesis for OBDC suffers a bit. Buying around the lows of the tariff sell off a month ago would have been easier.

For now, I would advise a wait and watch approach after a small starter "Buy" position, fully expecting dividend impacts and some NAV erosion in the event of a deeper crisis. The negatives of Q1 2025 were along expected lines, and I am initiating a buy given the fact that OBDC is relatively conservative and has a strong performance track record. I am also expecting the dividend income to stay reasonable despite potential cuts. Ensure the discount of at least 10% to NAV is maintained when entering, as prices react to Q1 2025 earnings.
